title: Claude Code Just Dropped /Goal. (Master it in 8 Minutes).
summary: Anthropic released the /goal command for Claude Code, a feature that allows users to specify a task and a completion criterion, then Claude executes it autonomously with a second AI verifying completion. The video demonstrates the feature with a live demo creating a content calendar for a pizza shop.
writeup: This video demonstrates Claude Code's /goal command, which lets a user specify a verifiable finish-line condition and then walk away while the tool runs autonomously turn after turn until the condition is satisfied. The mechanism is a dual-agent loop: a primary worker model (Opus or Sonnet) does the actual work, while a lightweight supervisor model evaluates after every step whether the goal has been met and either halts or restarts the worker with a progress report. The central lesson is that the quality of the finish-line description determines whether the loop converges cleanly or spins indefinitely burning tokens, making precise, checkable output conditions and an explicit turn or time cap essential hygiene.
key_points: The /goal command runs a dual-agent loop where a worker model builds or processes files and a second supervisor model checks after every turn whether the stated finish condition is met, continuing automatically until the supervisor confirms completion.

A finish-line condition must describe a concrete, verifiable output state (a specific named file, a folder structure, a CSV with one row per receipt) rather than a qualitative directive like 'make everything organized,' because the supervisor can only confirm what the worker reports back and cannot independently audit vague criteria.

Auto-approve is a separate toggle from /goal; without it, Claude pauses and requests permission before every file edit or command, so truly hands-off operation requires explicitly enabling auto-approve while understanding what system access that grants.

Appending 'stop after N turns' or 'stop after X minutes' to the goal condition is a mandatory safety cap: without it, a poorly specified finish line can cause the loop to run indefinitely, exhausting the daily token allowance or accumulating real API costs.
Typing /goal with no arguments displays an active-run dashboard showing elapsed time, turn count, and tokens consumed, giving a live view into the autonomous run without interrupting it.

The /usage command checks remaining credits before starting a long autonomous run, and the recommended cost discipline is to start with a small, bounded task (one folder, one batch of receipts) rather than a project-wide reorganization until the finish-line authoring pattern is practiced.
